coredata.dc:description: Currently, photocatalytic degradation is a fascinating technology due to its capability to treat escalating environmental problems by degrading chemicals like dyes and pharmaceuticals from wastewater. Herein, the hexagonal prism GQDs/h-MoO3 composite photocatalyst was fabricated through a facile hydrothermal approach. The synthesized samples were identified by XRD, SEM, FT-IR, XPS, UV-Vis DRS, and PL technologies to study the crystal structure, morphology, surface chemical composition, and optical properties. The preparation circumstances of binary compounds, such as different GQDs (Graphene quantum dots) loading amounts, precursor solution pH, hydrothermal temperature, and hydrothermal time were optimized, also, and the photocatalytic activity of materials is determined by photodegradation of Methylene Blue in this approach, which systematically investigated the impact of operational conditions on the efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction. The results revealed that 10GQDs/h-MoO3 has the best photocatalytic performance among competitors, which exhibited a high photodegradation efficiency (85.58%) of MB, and the rate was approximately 2.13 times greater than pristine h-MoO3. Moreover, the PL and EIS measurements proved the outstanding separation and migration ability of the photo-excited carriers of composite, which was attributable to the fact that GQDs can serve as electron transporters and acceptors to induce electron-hole separation efficiently. Finally, the free radical scavenging experiments confirmed that the primary active species was h+ in the photocatalytic process, as well as a potential explanation for the improved photocatalytic activity of GQDs/h-MoO3 photocatalysts was proposed.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots modified hexagonal prisms h-MoO3 for enhanced photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue under UV light irradiation

ce:author.ce:contributor-role: Methodology, Writing – original craft, Writing – review & editing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, Chongqing 401331, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University of Science and Technology, Chongqing, 401331, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Currently, photocatalytic degradation is a fascinating technology due to its capability to treat escalating environmental problems by degrading chemicals like dyes and pharmaceuticals from wastewater. Herein, the hexagonal prism GQDs/h-MoO3 composite photocatalyst was fabricated through a facile hydrothermal approach. The synthesized samples were identified by XRD, SEM, FT-IR, XPS, UV-Vis DRS, and PL technologies to study the crystal structure, morphology, surface chemical composition, and optical properties. The preparation circumstances of binary compounds, such as different GQDs (Graphene quantum dots) loading amounts, precursor solution pH, hydrothermal temperature, and hydrothermal time were optimized, also, and the photocatalytic activity of materials is determined by photodegradation of Methylene Blue in this approach, which systematically investigated the impact of operational conditions on the efficiency of the photocatalytic reaction. The results revealed that 10GQDs/h-MoO3 has the best photocatalytic performance among competitors, which exhibited a high photodegradation efficiency (85.58%) of MB, and the rate was approximately 2.13 times greater than pristine h-MoO3. Moreover, the PL and EIS measurements proved the outstanding separation and migration ability of the photo-excited carriers of composite, which was attributable to the fact that GQDs can serve as electron transporters and acceptors to induce electron-hole separation efficiently. Finally, the free radical scavenging experiments confirmed that the primary active species was h+ in the photocatalytic process, as well as a potential explanation for the improved photocatalytic activity of GQDs/h-MoO3 photocatalysts was proposed.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Rapid urban and industrial growth in recent decades has resulted in severe water resource constraints and water environment contamination. Organic pollutants coming from petrochemical, textile, dyeing, and other industries can spread swiftly through the aquatic environment due to their extremely soluble characteristics [1,2], which posed serious hazards to both human and animal existence owing to their toxicity and genotoxicity, so researchers have concentrated on potential strategies to remove harmful organic pollutants from tainted water using a variety of biological, physical, and chemical techniques [3,4].

Because of its tremendous potential in environmental cleanup and water purification technology, the field of photocatalysis has received extensive attention. Photocatalysis is considered for or dye degradation based on the interaction between light and semiconductor to generate reactive oxidation species (ROS), including hydroxyl and oxygen peroxide radicals that can be utilized to oxidize toxic pollutants to CO2, H2O, and other inorganic substances [5,6]. Photocatalytic dye degradation is recognized as one of the most promising approaches because of its excellent characteristics, including minimal price, high performance, lower power consumption, reasonable condition, wide applicability, and no subsequent pollution [7–9].

Developing new and highly active photocatalysts is a key point of photocatalysis technology, till date, for the photocatalytic reaction, a diversity of catalysts have been employed, including metals, nonmetallic, and their oxides. Among the reported photocatalysts, transition metal oxides such as h-MoO3 are known to be a typical n-type semiconductor with unique energetic and electrical properties, one-dimensional tunnel structure, and excellent physicochemical characteristics [10–13], and it has attracted a lot of interest as a prime candidate for photocatalyst. Regrettably, according to the report, the photocatalytic performance of h-MoO3 is decreased due to poor charge carrier separation and migration as well as weak photoresponse [14], these are hindrances in the practical application of h-MoO3, to address the demerits, compounding [15–18], morphology regulation [19] as well as doping [5,20] are used as prevalent modification methods to facilitate charge separation, emigration, and light collecting.

Recently, Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted people's interest due to their distinctive physical and chemical characteristics [21]. First, GQDs is a novel class of Zero-dimensional (0D) carbon material with small (less than 10 nm) graphene nanosheets which possess evident quantum confinement effect and fringe effect [22–25]. Second, compared to ordinary quantum dots, GQDs have some appealing qualities, including superior biocompatibility, photoelectric characteristics, minimal toxicity, and a straightforward manufacturing method [26–28], especially, Graphene quantum dots have become a research hotspot in the field of photocatalysis due to their high efficiency of charge storage and electron transfer and remarkable photoluminescence [29–31], the several GQDs based photocatalysts have been equipped, like as GQDs/CeO2 [32], N-Bi2O2CO3/GQDs [33], GQDs/Bi2S3 [34], GQDs/TiO2 [35] and so forth, which may serve as a solid-state electron transport mediator in the photocatalyst device, additionally, the electrical and optical properties of GQDs can be tuned to make it more effective by simply modifying heteroatoms such as nitrogen, sulfur, boron, phosphorus, etc. [36–38]. Hence, the N, S co-doped GQDs for decorating h-MoO3 appear to be a perfect strategy for improving the separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers.

Based on the above background, in this work, we synthesized GQDs-decorated h-MoO3 hexagonal prisms composite utilizing a viable hydrothermal approach for photocatalytic dye removal. Meanwhile, the preparation conditions of the composite such as GQDs loading amounts, precursor solution pH, hydrothermal temperature, and time were optimized. The structure, morphology, and surface chemical component of produced samples were then thoroughly analyzed using a variety of analytical techniques. Subsequently, the conditions of the photocatalytic process, which included the beginning dye concentration, catalyst dose, original pH value, and UV lamp power, are then methodically investigated. Compared to pure h-MoO3, the as-prepared GQDs modified h-MoO3 (GQDs/h-MoO3) exhibited better performance in the photocatalytic degradation of MB with remarkably enhanced efficiency. Finally, based on PL and EIS measurements, an active substance capture experiment, and the analysis of energy band structure, furthermore discussed a potential mechanism of GQDs enhanced photocatalytic activity.

Anhydrous ethanol (C2H6O, 99.7%), sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS, 90.0%), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.0%), ammonium oxalate (AO, 99.5%), silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99.8%), ascorbic acid (AA, 99.7%), ammonium heptamolybdate tetrahydrate (AHM, 99.0%) and methylene blue (MB, 98.5%) were purchased from Cologne Chengdu Chemical Reagent Company. Nitric acid (HNO3, 65.0∼68.0%), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 36.0~38.0%), and isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 99.7%) were purchased from Chongqing Chuandong Chemical Co., LTD. Thiourea (CH4N2S, 99.0%) from Aladdin. Sodium citrate (C6H5Na3O7, 99.0%) from McLean. All substances are analytical reagent grade and can be used without further purification. All solutions are made of ultra-pure water.

GQDs were synthesized by modifying the procedure reported in the literature [39]. Briefly, GQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method with melamine as raw material. To create a homogenous and transparent mixed solution, 70 mL of deionized water was first mixed well for 1 h before adding a 1:1 molar ratio of trisodium citrate and thioureone. After heating the combined solution for 5 h at 200 °C in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel reaction vessel, moreover, the reactor was cooled down to room temperature, and the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane to produce a suspended solution of N and S doped brown yellow graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Finally, the resultant solution was refrigerated for subsequent use.

According to literature reports [19], h-MoO3 was synthesized by hydrothermal method with AHM as raw material. In a typical process, ammonium paramolybdate was weighed and added into 50 mL ultrapure water, followed by magnetically stirring for 60 min to dissolve and form a uniform solution. Then, 4 mol/L nitric acid solution was gradually added while magnetic stirring the mixture to adjust pH to 1, and the precursor solution was further stirred to obtain. Next, the precursor solution was converted into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel reactor, the reactor was then placed in the oven at 160 °C for 24 h. After the hydrothermal reaction, the residual powder products in the reactor were cooled to ambient temperature and periodically rinsed with ultrapure water and anhydrous ethanol several times. Eventually, the centrifuged products were deposited in an oven at 90 °C for 8 h to produce h-MoO3.

GQDs/h-MoO3 binary composites were prepared by hydrothermal method, as shown in Scheme 1. Here are the specific steps: Initially, 1 mL, 3 mL, 5 mL, 8 mL, and 10 mL GQDs solutions were measured and diluted to 50 mL using deionized water, respectively, the solution was then mixed with 2.5 mmol AHM as raw material and 0.2 mmol sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate as a surfactant, respectively, and magnetically agitated for 60 min to create a homogenous solution. Subsequently, 4 mol/L nitric acid solution was gently added to the solution under magnetic stirring to set the pH value to 1, and the precursor solution mixed with GQDs was obtained by fully stirring evenly, that is, the precursor of binary composites. The precursor solution was positioned in a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel high-pressure reactor and heated in an oven to 160 °C for 24 h before cooling to room temperature. The remaining powder in the reactor was removed from the reactor and centrifuged with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol 2–3 times. The centrifuged products were placed in a constant temperature drying oven at 90 °C for 8 h, and a series of GQDs/h-MoO3 binary composite catalysts were obtained. These catalysts were denoted as XGM (X = 1, 3, 5, 8, 10).

After obtaining the optimal GQDs loading amount through the degradation of MB, AHM and the optimal GQDs loading amount were mixed, and the precursor solution is prepared at pH of 1, then this precursor was thermally reacted at different temperatures for 24 h (100 °C, 120 °C, 140 °C, 150 °C, 160 °C), the final obtained catalysts were denoted as XGM-Y (Y=100, 120, 140, 150, 160). After that, under optimal GQDs loading amount and hydrothermal temperature conditions, a series of composites were prepared by the same method at different hydrothermal times (3 h, 8 h, 12 h, 18 h, 24 h, 28 h, 33 h), these catalysts were denoted as Z-XGM-Y (Z = 3, 8, 12, 18, 24, 28, 33). Similarly, under optimal GQDs loading amount, the precursor solution is prepared at different pH values by controlling the amount of nitric acid, then this precursor was thermally reacted at optimized hydrothermal temperature and time, and the final obtained catalysts were denoted as M-Z-XGM-Y (M=0.4, 0.7, 1, 1.2, 1.5), where M represents the precursor solution pH, Z represents the hydrothermal time, X represents the number of GQDs, and Y represents the hydrothermal temperature. The names of all prepared samples are shown in Table 1.

XRD-7000 (Japan; Cu-K, 45 kV, 30 mA) was utilized to characterize the crystal phase of the samples as prepared. The morphology of synthesized materials was examined using SEM (JSM-7800 F, Japan), and their elemental makeup and chemical state were determined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fischer, ESCALAB Xi+). The microstructure was observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM, Talos F200S G2). The spectra from an FT-IR spectrometer (Tensor-27, Germany) were applied to determine the functional groups of the samples. The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (T9, China) and the photoluminescence (PL) spectrometer (Hitachi F-4600) were employed to determine the optical characteristics of materials. A three-electrode system on the electrochemical workstation was used to undertake the EIS measurements (Autolab PGSTAT 302Ns).

The photocatalytic degradation performances of all prepared catalysts were evaluated by using Methylene Blue as a representative water pollutant and a 25 W ultraviolet light with a 254 nm emission wavelength was utilized as the source of exposure to radiation. Specifically, a quartz pipe that was located 5 cm from the light source received additions of a specific quantity of prepared photocatalyst and a liquid of Methylene Blue. The solid-liquid photocatalytic mixture was stirred by blowing air for 40 min in the absence of light prior to the photoreaction in order to achieve the adsorption-desorption balance that exists at normal temperature. The suspension was then subjected to ultraviolet light at room temperature for a predetermined amount of time, a 5 mL reaction solution was removed on a regular basis, and the surplus powder was removed by centrifuging the mixture. Following that, a UV–vis spectrophotometer was utilized to determine the absorbance value of the supernatant Methylene Blue solution at its maximum absorption wavelength (664 nm). The removal rate of the Methylene Blue liquid could then be determined by employing the equation R= (1-C/C0)× 100%, where C represents the UV absorbance post photocatalytic reaction and C0 represents the absorbance of the beginning solution.

All the DFT calculations were carried out in the Cambridge Sequential Total Energy Pack (CASTEP) module with a cutoff energy of 260 eV and complete relaxation of all atoms in the cell, the relaxation convergence accuracy is 1 × 5−5 eV/atom, the convergence accuracy of the interatomic force field is set to 1 eV/nm, the maximum displacement is limited to 5 × 10−4 nm, while the maximum stress is fixed at 0.2 GPa. Subsequently, in the first Brillouin zone, 1 × 1 × 1 Monkhorst-Pack lattice sampling is performed on K points for self-consistent calculations. By referring to XRD analysis, the geometrical structure of hexagonal MoO3 (space group PNMA) cells was optimized by the GGA-PBE method. Lattice constants were a= 3.76 Å, b= 2.97 Å, and c= 14.43 Å. The calculated data are consistent with the experimental values [19].

The crystal structures of pure h-MoO3 and GQDs/h-MoO3 (1-24-10GM-160) composite catalysts were analyzed by XRD. Fig. 1 a demonstrates the distinctive diffraction peaks of h-MoO3 at 16.66°, 19.30°, 25.62°, 29.31°, 33.94°, 35.24°, 43.04°, 45.41°, 46.46°, and 55.95° correspond to (110), (200), (210), (300), (220), (310), (320), (410), (404) and (218) crystal surface of hexagonal phase molybdenum trioxide, respectively, which has a good match with the PDF standard card (JCPDS NO. 21-0569). It was found that the phase of the GQDs/h-MoO3 catalyst hardly changed, but compared with pure h-MoO3, the diffraction peak intensity became stronger, especially the characteristic peaks of (300) and (310) crystal planes were more obvious, indicating that the introduction of GQDs would affect the crystal orientation of h-MoO3 [40]. Besides that, the XRD pattern did not reveal the characteristic diffraction peak of GQDs (26°), which may be a result of the low quantity and crystallinity of GQDs, so the new diffraction peaks superimposed or condensed during the synthesis of graphene quantum dots failed to show up, which is similar to many related pieces of literature reports [26,41–44]. The crystal size and strain were calculated by the Williamson-Hall method. Williamson-Hall (W-H) equation is obtained by the following formula [45]:

. The slope in the W-H plot shows the type of lattice strain made in the sample. The negative slop represents compressive strain and the positive slop means tensile strain [79]. According to Fig. 1b-c, the crystal size of h-MoO3 is 59.25 nm and the strain is 0.0019, while the crystal size of GQDs/h-MoO3 is 81.08 nm and the strain is 0.0011.

The FT-IR spectra of pure h-MoO3 and GQDs/h-MoO3 composite catalysts are shown in Fig. 1d. The figure shows that for pure h-MoO3, the bending vibration absorption peak of the O-Mo-O bond is at 516 cm−1, and the stretching vibration absorption peaks are at 910 cm−1 and 976 cm−1. Whereas the absorption peaks at 1405 cm−1 and 1638 cm−1 are ascribed to the bending vibration of H-O-H and O-H groups, respectively, the wide peaks near 3450 cm−1 are due to the stretching vibration absorption peaks of water molecules adsorbed on the surface of h-MoO3 [12]. It can be seen from the graphs of composite materials prepared at 150 ℃ and 160 ℃ hydrothermal temperatures after the introduction of GQDs, the characteristic peak strength of the O-Mo-O bond at 516 cm−1 becomes very weak. Besides, a noticeable N-H bond stretching vibration peak is saw at 3100–3200 cm−1, and the less explicit C-O single bond or CS stretching vibration peak appeared near 1100 cm−1, which may be from the graphene quantum dots modified by N and S. Overall, GQDs hardly changed the functional group structure of h-MoO3.

The FT-IR spectrum of the GQDs solution were shown in Fig. 1e, it can be observed that the peak around 1080 cm−1 is the stretching vibration absorption peak of the C-O bond in GQDs[31], the peak around 1110 cm−1 is the stretching vibration absorption peak of CS bond, and the characteristic signal near 1400 cm−1 are of the stretching vibration of SO and C-N bonds [26,46]. The CC functional group in GQDs can be observed in the absorbance peak at 1580 cm−1, the tensile vibration of the cumulative double bond N = CS is represented by the absorption peak at 2060 cm−1, and the flexural vibration of O-H and the tensile vibration of CO are represented by the absorption peak at 1640 cm−1, in the range of 3100–3500 cm−1, there is a large absorption peak attributed to the tensile vibration of O-H and N-H functional groups [31,47]. This outcome validated the generation of graphene quantum dots with N and S modifications.

In Fig. 2, the SEM pictures of photocatalytic materials were exhibited. Fig. 2a and 2b show the SEM images of pure h-MoO3 and GQDs/h-MoO3 after magnification by 3000 times, respectively. According to the parameters of this experiment, as can be observed from Fig. 2a, the size of pure h-MoO3 generated by the hydrothermal approach is not homogeneous., most of them exist in hexagonal rod-like morphology, with a few irregular blocks, and pure h-MoO3 is composed of hexagonal rod aggregates with a microscopic size of about 30–50 µm. It is clearly demonstrated in Fig. 2b, the GQDs/h-MoO3 composite shows a very regular hexagonal prism structure, the material size is about 10–30 µm, the introduction of GQDs and the use of sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate as surfactant have a great impact on the morphology of h-MoO3. Moreover, the molybdate (VI) nucleation rate is slowed down due to the high concentration of the raw material ammonium molybdate solution utilized in this study, which also increases the likelihood of crystal nuclei colliding. As a result, part of Mo (Ⅵ) is firstly nucleated and preferentially develops into a hexagonal prism structure and keeps growing, so the hexagonal prism is in disarray and of uneven size, and the immature grains are attached to the hexagonal prism. Fig. 2c is the TEM image of GQDs/h-MoO3. The results show that GQDs are scattered small spherical black dots with an average size of 5 nm, which are evenly distributed on the surface of GQDs/h-MoO3. Therefore, it can be inferred that GQDs and h-MoO3 are successfully coupled. The HR-TEM image is shown in Fig. 2d. The measured lattice plane spacing of 0.308 nm is attributed to the (002) plane of GQDs, which further confirms the successful introduction of GQDs into h-MoO3 [24]. Furthermore, the EDS-Mapping (Fig. 2e) revealed that the GQDs/h-MoO3 sample contained Mo, O, C, N, and S elements.

The chemical makeup of the surface and the electronic condition of the GQDs/h-MoO3 composite were investigated by XPS, the survey scan XPS spectrum, shown in Fig. 3a, clearly demonstrates that the elements Mo, O, C, N, and S are detected in GQDs/h-MoO3 samples, indicating that GQDs is successfully loaded on h-MoO3. Fig. 3b shows the XPS fine spectrum of the Mo element in the GQDs/h-MoO3 composite, at the binding energies of 232.8 eV and 235.9 eV, two distinct peaks that correspond to the Mo 3d5/2 and Mo 3d3/2 orbitals, respectively, can be seen[9], proving that Mo is present in the compound in the oxidized form of Mo6+. Fig. 3c is a high-resolution figure of O 1 s, molybdenum trioxide's lattice oxygen is responsible for the peaks with binding energies of 530.8 eV, 531.5 eV, and 532.2 eV, the O-H bond of GQDs and the oxygen in H2O adsorbed on the catalyst surface, respectively[48]. The XPS high-resolution map obtained by C 1s fitting is shown in Fig. 3d, which is mainly divided into three carbon species, at the binding energy of 284.8 eV, the characteristic peaks of C = C and C-C with sp2 hybrid orbitals in GQD correspond to the absorption peaks at 285.8 eV, which belong to the functional groups C-O, C-S and C-N of sp3 hybridization, and the CO group in GQDs is represented by the peak at 288.6 eV[43]. In the high-resolution picture of N 1 s, two peaks at binding energies of 398.8 eV and 401.8 eV can be seen (Fig. 3e), which are respectively attributed to pyrrole nitrogen (C-N-C) and graphite nitrogen[30]. Moreover, there are three distinct peaks in the high-resolution image at S 2p, including the S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 electrons matching to C-S at 163.8 eV and 164.5 eV, and the peak at 168 eV belong to the SO group in the modified GQDs (Fig. 3f) [49,50]. These findings verified that the hydrothermal reaction procedure used to generate the composite catalyst of GQDs/h-MoO3 was successful.

By using UV-Vis DRS, the light response characteristics of pure h-MoO3 and GQDs/h-MoO3 were assessed. As shown in Fig. 4a, pure h-MoO3 shows absorption characteristics in the UV light and part of the visible light range, and its absorption boundary is near 420 nm. This results from h-MoO3 electrons moving effectively from the valence band to the conduction band, the conduction band (LUMO level) and valence band (HOMO level) are primarily produced via the hybridization of the O 2p and Mo 4d orbitals in h-MoO3, respectively[6]. Compared with pure h-MoO3, the absorption edge of the GQDs/h-MoO3 composite has a certain degree of redshift while the light absorption intensity of the composite has dramatically increased. This indicates that the addition of GQDs can effectively increase a light absorption range of material, and improve the utilization of light, which also increase the capacity of material for responding to light within that range and facilitate the composite harvesting of more photons, thereby increasing the number of photogenerated electron-hole pairs that take part in the photocatalytic process. The band gap width (Eg) of pure h-MoO3 and GQDs/h-MoO3 is obtained by the Tauc plot method. As shown in Fig. 4b, from the (αhv)1/2-hv relationship, according to calculations, the band gaps of h-MoO3 and GQDs/h-MoO3 are 3.32 eV and 3.16 eV, respectively. Therefore, GQDs/h-MoO3 can absorb photons with lower energy in long wavelengths for photocatalytic reaction.

UV–vis and PL measurements were used to examine the optical properties of GQDs. Fig. 5a shows that GQDs display noticeable ultraviolet light absorption in the 200–400 nm range. The first peak near 233 nm belongs to the π-π * electronic transition of graphite domain sp2 in GQDs, corresponding to the CC group. The presence of oxygen, nitrogen, and sulfur in the structure of graphite results in the n-π * electronic transition, which is responsible for the other absorption band located close to 338 nm[47]. Fig. 5b shows the PL spectra of GQDs at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. They form a structure resembling graphite, which is essentially comparable to the previous descriptions. It is obvious to observe that the maximum emission wavelength of the GQDs solution is about 440 nm. In addition, in the illustration of Fig. 5a, GQDs solution appears as a clear brown-yellow liquid under natural light, while in the illustration of Fig. 5b, when exposed to ultraviolet light with a wavelength of 356 nm, GQDs solution generates blue light; this blue photoluminescence is most likely caused by the localization of electron-hole pairs, a previously described phenomenon[46,49,50], combined with FT-IR spectra of GQDs solution, the above results show that the quantum carbon nanoparticles of GQDs were successfully prepared in this study.

The degradation of organic dye MB was utilized to assess the photocatalytic properties of as-prepared samples under UV light (254 nm, 25 W) for identifying the ideal photocatalyst synthesis conditions. The photocatalytic reaction in this experiment took place for 120 min at room temperature after 0.05 g of catalyst was introduced to 100 mL of 25 mg/L MB. The effect of the GQDs loading quantities on photocatalytic activity is displayed in Fig. 6a, the removal rate of MB under the conditions of pure h-MoO3 and with only UV light is just 38.45% and 6.02%, respectively; however, as the concentration of graphene quantum dots increases gradually, the removal rate of MB also rises. The removal rate of MB increases to a high of 85.58% when 10 mL of GQDs solution is added, which is 2.23 times and 14.21 times greater than under the circumstances of pure h-MoO3 and solely UV light, respectively. The work demonstrates that doping GQDs can considerably increase the photocatalytic activity of composite, which may be due to the ability of GQDs to quickly separate photogenerated electron-hole pairs. The GQDs/h-MoO3 exhibit a diffraction mode similar to that of pure h-MoO3 under different doping amounts of GQDs, with sharp peak shape and good crystallinity in the XRD pattern (Fig. 6b), after doping GQDs, the intensity of h-MoO3 diffraction peak changes, indicating that its crystal orientation is affected to some extent. And it can be observed from the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 6c) that when the doping amount of GQDs solution is 10 mL, the peak intensity of the O-Mo-O bond absorption peak at 516 cm−1 becomes very weak, but it hardly affects the functional group structure of h-MoO3. As shown in Fig. 6d, the elimination rate of MB increased progressively with the increase in calcination temperature, reaching a peak degradation rate of 85.58% at the temperature of 160 °C, and it can be seen from the XRD pattern. The influence of hydrothermal temperature on the photocatalytic property was explored (Fig. 6e), the crystal structure of the composite hardly changes in the hydrothermal temperature range of 100–160 °C, it can be observed from the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 6f) that there is a clear N-H bond stretching vibration peak at 3150 cm-1 when the temperature approaches 150 and 160 °C, and there is a faint C-O single bond or CS stretching vibration peak at 1100 cm-1, which could emanate from the GQDs. As demonstrated in Fig. 6g, the impact of hydrothermal duration on the photocatalytic performance was also examined, with the extension of hydrothermal time, the MB removal rate showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing. The rate of MB removal was 85.58% when the hydrothermal time was 24 h; unfortunately, when the time was increased to 28 h and 33 h, the rates fell to 74.61% and 69.79%, respectively. As a result, the long hydrothermal time did not help improve the photocatalytic properties of the composite. In addition, as shown in Fig. 6h and Fig. 6i, which show the XRD patterns and FT-IR spectra of the specimens produced at various hydrothermal times, respectively, the characteristic peak positions of all samples were consistent with the results of earlier characterization. The impact of precursor solution pH on photocatalytic performance is depicted in Fig. 6j. It can be seen that when the dosage of catalytic material is 0.03 g and 0.05 g, the photocatalytic degradation rate of MB decreases with the decrease of pH value. When pH= 0.4 and 0.7, the degradation rate of MB at 0.05 g is only 55.31% and 64.97%, which proves that the higher the acidity, the lower the photocatalytic activity of the GQDs/h-MoO3 binary composite. During the experiment, it was found that when pH= 1.2 and 1.5, a small amount of black unknown solid was obtained after hydrothermal reaction, washing, and centrifugation, and during the hydrothermal reaction, no solid material was produced when the pH climbed to 2. Fig. 6k and Fig. 6l are the FT-IR and XRD spectra of GQDs/h-MoO3 prepared at different precursor solution pH respectively. When pH is 1.2 and 1.5, in contrast, the structures of materials have significantly changed and are no longer the original compounds. Fig. 6m is the XRD magnification of the substances obtained at pH= 1.2 and 1.5. XRD analysis shows that the material prepared is the intermediate products ammonium tetramolybdate and ammonium trimolybdate from the raw material AHM (ammonium heptamolybdate) and ammonium paramolybdate in the presence of nitric acid. In the XRD spectra, (NH4)6Mo7O24·4 H2O (PDF#27–1013), (NH4)6Mo7O24 (PDF#23–0784), (NH4)2Mo3O10·H2O (PDF#39–0654), (NH4)2Mo4O13 (PDF#50–0608) respectively match the corresponding PDF standard cards well. The reason for this phenomenon is that when the content of H+ is insufficient, the reaction with ammonium heptamolybdate is incomplete, and the only states in which the products can exist are the intermediate forms of ammonium trimolybdate and ammonium tetramolybdate, which are not favorable for producing the desired product, h-MoO3.

Using GQDs/h-MoO3 (1-24-10GM-160) as a photocatalyst, the effects of operational factors such as the beginning concentration of the dye, catalyst dose, solution pH value, and UV lamp power were investigated in order to establish the best operating parameters for MB degradation. Fig. 7a illustrates how varying beginning MB concentrations affect the rate of MB removal, indicating that when the initial concentration is 10 mg/L, the MB removal rate reached about 91.59% after 120 min. The removal percentage, nevertheless, gradually declines as the original concentration rises from 10 to 60 mg/L, and the removal rate was only 43.85% at a large starting concentration of MB (60 mg/L) even after 150 min of degradation, which may be as a result of too many MB molecules prevented the transmission of light, leading to inadequate degradation. Thereafter, the pseudo-first-order kinetic equation ln(C0/C) =kt was employed to estimate the parameters of the degradation process and examine the degradation kinetics. Fig. 7b presented the apparent reaction rate decreased with the increase of the initial concentration of MB solution, with an initial MB concentration of 10 mg/L, the reaction system has a maximum K value of 0.0125 min−1, and the values of the correlation coefficients R2 for each group are near to 1, indicating that the photodegradation process follows the pseudo-first-order kinetic model. As shown in Fig. 7c, the impact of catalyst dosage on the removal efficiency of MB within 3 h. GQDs/h-MoO3 were dosed at a rate of 0.01–0.09 g. The MB elimination rate gradually rose from 34.50% to 97.53% with the increase in catalyst concentration after 150 min of degradation by 100 mL of 25 mg/L MB. By simulating the experimental photocatalysis results (Fig. 7d), it can be observed that the degradation effect is worst at concentrations of 0.1 g/L, where k equals 0.0019 min−1. The reaction rate constant k rises to 0.0259 min−1 when the concentration is increased to 0.9 g/L, which is higher than the system under other catalyst concentrations, this is due to the fact that the introduction of a catalyst can improve the effective interaction of catalytic material with the dye and that UV light can excite additional electrons, holes, and other active substances to partake in the photocatalytic reaction. The pH value is an essential operating variable in wastewater treatment. The effect of pH on the rate of MB removal was seen in Fig. 7e. HCl or NaOH solution was used to change the starting pH of MB solution, in this experiment, 0.05 g of catalyst was placed in 100 mL of a 25 mg/L MB solution. It is evident that the photocatalytic degradation of composite material of MB has attained a percentage removal of even more than 80% in a broad pH range of 3.1–9.5, which is advantageous for the practical use of the material in stain photocatalytic degradation. However, when the pH is adjusted to 11.5, the photocatalytic degradation rate of MB of the material is only 53.54%, besides that, Fig. 7f demonstrated that when pH is 4, the composite reaches the highest reaction rate constant k of 0.0103 min−1, the adsorption process is an important pre-step in photocatalysis, which mainly depends on the charge-controlled electrostatic interaction between the catalyst surface and the degradation substance because the charge-distribution characteristics of the catalyst surface are determined by the pH of medium[51,52]. In order to better explain the electrostatic interaction between GQDs/h-MoO3 composite and MB at various starting pH levels, the surface charge of GQDs/h-MoO3 binary composite in different pH solutions was characterized by Zeta Potential measurement. Fig. 7g presents the findings. As can be observed, the surface of the material is positively charged when the pH of the solution becomes less than 1.8 as well as negatively charged when the pH of the solution is higher than 1.8. As we all know, MB is a cationic dye, so the electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged material and MB is conducive to adsorption and degradation in a wide pH range. When the solution alkaline is increased to pH=11.5, the degradation rate is only 0.0026 min−1. Although the pH of the medium is favorable for adsorption, the peralkaline catalyst with negative charges will adsorb photogenic holes and inhibit the generation of •OH. Therefore, it is not conducive to the degradation of MB by GQDs/h-MoO3 under this condition. In accordance with Fig. 7h, the wattage of the UV lamp is another significant factor impacting the photocatalytic performance of samples, with the growth of light intensity, the degradation rate of MB also increased, and after 150 min photocatalytic degradation, the removal rate of MB under 25 W and 50 W light reached 85.66% and 90.10%, respectively, and when the intensity increased to 75 W, the removal rate reached 87.37% only after 90 min. The photoreaction time was reduced greatly, and the degradation rate was 96.74% at 150 min, which is apparent in Fig. 7i, where the rate of the reaction constant k arrived at its highest value of 0.0211 min−1 at 75 W since more electrons are excited per unit of time on the exterior of GQDs/h-MoO3 as UV lamp intensity increases, it is advantageous for the emergence of photogenerated electrons and holes. In order to further investigate the widely effective application of GQDs/h-MoO3 photocatalyst for degradation of dye wastewater, seven commonly used dyes including Acid Red B, Crystal violet, Neutral red, Carmine, Eriochrome black T, Congo red, and Rhodamine B were selected as target degraders for photocatalytic experiments. The degradation rates for the common dye Rhodamine B were 93.49% in Fig. 7j, and the degradation rate of the composite of crystal violet and chrome black T, two dark dyes, is 97.12% and 95.26%, respectively, and the degradation rates for Acid Red B, neutral red, carmine red, and Congo red were 58.31%, 93.39%, 62.75%, and 81.50%, respectively. These results indicate that GQDs/h-MoO3 composites can be considered potential photocatalysts for the photocatalytic decomposition of dye pollutants. In contrast, the degradability of carmine red and Acid Red B decreased, and the structures of diverse dye molecules, redox potentials, and photosensitivity may be to blame for the various degradation consequences. In addition, it is worth noting that the photocatalytic performance of the developed GQDs/h-MoO3 composite photocatalyst was compared with previously reported photocatalysts. The results of this comparison, listed in Table 2, demonstrated that the prepared GQDs/h-MoO3 has good MB removal capacity at relatively lower doses and with less energy consumption, suggesting that GQDs/h-MoO3 can be used as an effective UV-driven photocatalyst for methylene blue degradation.

Experiments were conducted to learn more about the potential degrading mechanism of binary composite CS. The fluctuation of the UV–vis spectra of MB solution during the photodegradation process was shown in Fig. 8a at various intervals, there are two aggregation forms of monomer and dimer belonging to MB solution in the strong absorption band at 664 nm, and the acromion near 611 nm, and the π→π * transition of the benzene ring is what is responsible for the absorption peak at 293 nm[13]. The strength of these peak positions steadily declined as the reaction went on, and the corresponding characteristic peaks at 664 nm and 611 nm basically disappeared after 240 min of reaction, indicating that MB has been gradually degraded and its chromophores have been completely destroyed, in the same manner, a fresh absorption peak was clearly seen in the near ultraviolet area about 200 nm, demonstrating that the fundamental structure of MB molecule has nearly totally been destroyed but that the ensuing organic small molecules are not completely broken down. According to Fig. 8b, measurements of COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand) variations in MB solution during degrading were also made. The COD removal rate of MB solution gradually increased with the extension of photocatalytic reaction time, and the COD removal rate of MB solution reached 67.04%, indicating that most MB was decomposed by mineralization within 150 min. In comparison, the color removal rate and the COD elimination rate did differ to some extent. It's possible that the active ingredient can convert the chromophores into colorless molecules early on, even if the fundamental structure of MB molecules was ultimately shattered, there are still tiny molecules in the shape of organic matter that haven't fully mineralized.

The primary species that contribute to the breakdown of MB were identified through a free radical capture trial. Various scavengers, including isopropyl alcohol (IPA), ammonium oxalate (AO), ascorbic acid (AA), and silver nitrate (AgNO3), were used to capture the •OH, h+, •O2 -, and e- radicals, respectively, following dark adsorption, 0.1 mmol of each kind of scavenger is mixed into the MB reaction system, and the elimination rate of MB is gauged every 30 min. It is discernible from Fig. 9a and b demonstrate that the inclusion of AO considerably inhibits the photocatalytic degradation of MB, along with the addition of AgNO3 and IPA weakens the photocatalytic degradation process to some degree. This shows that the intermediate reactants in the reaction are e- and •OH, and despite the addition of AA, the reaction still exhibits a high rate of MB elimination. Fig. 9 a demonstrates this, the removal rate of MB sharply decreased by 37.20%, however, the elimination rate of MB only decreased by 1.31% in the influence of AA, which hardly changed the photocatalytic performance, demonstrating that whereas •O2 - rarely contributed to the photocatalytic reaction, h+ had an important function in the reaction mechanism. As a consequence, it is implied that the order of four active species of contribution to the degradation of MB in this photocatalytic system is h+ >e- > •OH > •O2 -.

The electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) and photoluminescence (PL) experiments were conducted to evaluate the separation effectiveness of the photo-excited carrier in hopes of unraveling the mechanism of improved photocatalytic activity. The EIS analyses of the as-prepared samples are shown in Fig. 10a, the smaller the EIS radius is, the lower the migration difficulty of the photogenerated carrier[60]. In the spectrum, the radius of GQDs/h-MoO3 is smaller than pure h-MoO3, indicating that GQDs/h-MoO3 has a lower migration resistance, so the interface transfer rate of charge carriers is faster, this may be attributed to the superior electron transport characteristics of GQDs, moreover, an effective charge transfer channel can be provided by the interface touch between GQDs and h-MoO3 [61]. Fig. 10b displays the PL spectra of GQDs/h-MoO3 and h-MoO3 with an excitation wavelength of 310 nm, the GQDs/h-MoO3 composite has a lower fluorescence intensity at 376 nm and 466 nm than that of h-MoO3, and this suggests that the photogenerated carrier undergoes less recombine in the photocatalytic reaction of GQDs/h-MoO3, considerably improving the photocatalytic activity of the catalyst.

The electron band structure of GQDs/h-MoO3 composites in the microscopic domain is further studied by using DFT calculation. The built-and-optimized structural models of GQDs, h-MoO3, and GQDs/h-MoO3 are displayed in Fig. 11. h-MoO3 has a layered stacked structure, from which layered h-MoO3 is stripped, belonging to a hexagonal dense stack structure, with Mo atoms in the middle layer and O atoms in both upper and lower layers, forming an O-Mo-O sandwich structure (Fig. 11a). The coronene, which functions as its smallest unit, can be used to represent GQDs, which are polyaromatic particles [62–64]. Due to their outstanding qualities, such as optical, electrical, and optoelectrical, similar units have been widely employed as a calculation model in a variety of applications [65–68]. To streamline the calculation, GQDs composed of seven benzene rings modified with N, O, and S were constructed (Fig. 11b). To match the lattice of layered h-MoO3 and graphene quantum dots, the GQDs/h-MoO3 composite materials model was used to match the layered h-MoO3 supercells with graphene quantum dots with a periodic structure of 4 * 1 * 1 (Fig. 11c and d). To avoid periodic interactions, the vacuum layer thickness was set at 12 Å.

Using the GGA-PBE function, the energy band and total density of states (TDOS) of h-MoO3 were calculated, and the band gap of h-MoO3 was found to be 1.73 eV. The results dramatically differ from the experimental findings, most likely as a result of a flaw in the GGA-PBE function that affects transition metal calculations[69–71]. According to the calculation presented in Fig. 12a, since the conduction band minimum (CBM) and valence band maximum (VBM) of h-MoO3 is located at Y and U, respectively, it is an indirect band-gap semiconductor. Fig. 12b shows the projected state density (PDOS) of h-MoO3. The Mo 4d and O 2p orbitals combine to produce the CBM of h-MoO3, whereas the O 2p orbitals dominate the VBM with only a minor amount of O 2 s, suggesting that the photogenerated electrons in the h-MoO3 semiconductor may come from the 2p orbitals of the O atom and then transition to the 4d orbitals of the Mo atom. As a result of the maximum values of indirect band-gap semiconductor materials for the guide band bottom and a full band being at separate locations in the K-space, generating a half-full band necessitates both energy absorption and momentum modification [69,72]. This may result in the poor photocatalytic ability of h-MoO3. As demonstrated in Fig. 12c and Fig. d, compared with pure h-MoO3, the introduction of impurity levels by S 2p, N 2p, and C 2p states causes the conduction band of GQDs/h-MoO3 compounds to shift downward and the band gap to narrow. This bandgap structure helps to enhance electrical conductivity, with the energy required for the carrier transition reduced and the carrier concentration increased, thus enhancing photocatalytic performance[69,73].

The energy band structure of the photocatalyst is indispensable for the explanation of the photocatalytic mechanism. Thus, the valence band position of h-MoO3 was obtained through VB-XPS characterization, as shown in Fig. 13, the EVB of h-MoO3 was determined to be 3.25 eV, combined with UV–vis DRS analysis, the Eg of h-MoO3 was determined to be 3.32 eV. The conduction position (ECB) of h-MoO3 can be determined as − 0.07 eV using the empirical formula ECB=EVB-Eg. In view of previous reports, in order for the photogenerated electrons to have sufficient energy to reduce O2 to •O2 -, the conduction band position of the semiconductor needs to be more negative than the normal reduction potential of O2/•O2 - (−0.33 eV vs NHE), and in need for the photogenerated holes to be able to oxidize OH- to •OH, the valence band potential of h-MoO3 must be more positive than the typical reduction potential of •OH/OH- (2.38 eV vs NHE) [74], as a result, the photogenerated holes have a large oxidation capacity due to the valence band potential of h-MoO3, which produces •OH to breakdown contaminants. The valence band potential of h-MoO3 is more active than that of O2/•O2 -, so the photogenerated electrons have a weak reducing capacity, and are difficult to reduce O2. The conclusion derived from the free radical trapping experiment is consistent with the inference that •O2 - has no part in the photocatalytic reaction.

A potential mechanism of improved photocatalytic breakdown of MB by GQDs/h-MoO3 with UV light was hypothesized and presented in Fig. 14 based on the aforementioned experimental findings. Specifically, under UV illumination, photons excite electrons in the VB of h-MoO3 and cause them to transfer to the CB, generating photogenerated holes in the VB. According to the band analysis and free radical capture experiment, h+ has a strong oxidation capacity and plays an important role in a photocatalytic reaction as the main active species, which can not only directly participate in the molecular degradation of MB, but also oxidize the OH- in the reaction system to •OH, and •OH also plays a part in the photocatalytic degradation. Photogenerated electrons on the CB can also directly degrade MB molecules, and work with h+ and •OH to degrade MB molecules into CO2, H2O, and other small molecules. Given previous reports, GQDs loaded on h-MoO3 can act as electron collectors, transporters, and accelerators [22,75,76], providing a fast and effective electron transfer pathway for photocatalytic reactions, photogenerated electrons on h-MoO3 CB can be stored by GQDs [21,77,78], and hence, the carrier lifetime is extended and unwanted electron-hole pair recombination is prevented. Eventually, the efficiency of photogenerated carrier separation and migration is greatly improved, thereby the photocatalytic activity is boosted.

In summary, highly efficient photocatalysts of GQDs-modified hexagonal prisms h-MoO3 were successfully prepared via a facile hydrothermal process. A variety of characterization techniques are used to determine the structure, morphology, chemical composition, and optical properties of synthetic composites. Optimization experiment of preparation conditions revealed that GQDs/h-MoO3 (1-24-10GM-160) has the best photocatalytic performance on MB degradation among competitors, compared to pure h-MoO3, its clearance rate was almost 2.23 times higher. The outcomes of the free radical capture experiment further demonstrated that h+ was a major contributor to the photocatalytic reaction. Combined with energy band structure analysis, a possible mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic performance was elucidated, it was validated through the results of the UV–vis DRS, PL, EIS tests, and DFT calculation that the introduction of GQDs is responsible for the laser-catalyzed properties of the GQDs/h-MoO3 composites, which not only enhance light absorption but also serve as electron collector, transporters and accelerator lowering the migration resistance of charge carriers and facilitating the dissociation of electron-hole pairs, thereby effectively promoting the photocatalytic property of h-MoO3 prisms. As a result, this work demonstrated that for the purpose of green development, the GQDs/h-MoO3 composite could be a promising replacement for photocatalysts in the removal of dye contaminants from the aquatic environment.

Shiyue Hu: Methodology, Writing – original craft, Writing – review & editing. Junhong Liu: Software, Writing – review & editing. Xuanlan Luo: Investigation. Qiqi Shen: Validation. Yu Qin: Visualization. Haikun Hu: Supervision. Jinhai Yuan: Conceptualization, Date curation, Project administration. Shuangkou Chen: Resources. Di Xu: Funding acquisition.
